related_chapters.subject: Abernathy family
related_chapters.why: Du Bois grieves a child lost too soon; the Abernathy children describe their father's inconsolable grief and the void left by a death in the family.

related_chapters.subject: Bill Russell
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Widower Raises His Two Boys
related_chapters.why: The essay centers a father's tender devotion to his child; Russell recounts his widowed father raising two boys alone after the loss of their mother.

related_chapters.subject: Grace Hall Miller
related_chapters.chapter_title: He Shot Him in the Back Over a Cow
related_chapters.why: Du Bois writes from inside a family's sudden bereavement; Grace Hall Miller recounts the day her husband was shot and killed, leaving her to raise their children.

related_chapters.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
related_chapters.topic: A Mother's Death and an Early Start in School
related_chapters.why: The essay turns on a child severed from a parent by death; Burns describes a mother's death that forced her to grow up overnight as a young child.

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.part_title: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
related_chapters.why: Du Bois frames a household shaped by death and remaking; Terry recounts how her parents, each widowed by the flu, married and built a family of twelve.

related_chapters.subject: John Carlos
related_chapters.why: The essay weighs what a parent foregoes for a child's sake; Carlos recounts the cost his own family bore, including the Christmases his children went without.

top_voice.subject: Abernathy family
top_voice.reason: The children's account of their father's grief and the unfillable void most directly echoes the essay's portrait of a parent broken by loss within the family.

related_chapters.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
related_chapters.chapter_title: You Owed Your Soul to the Store
related_chapters.why: Du Bois anatomizes the crop-lien debt that bound the cotton tenant; Blake describes the sharecropping debt system in which a family owed its soul to the plantation store.

related_chapters.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
related_chapters.why: The essay studies the Black Belt tenant working another man's cotton on shares; Jones recounts farming cotton, corn, and peas for a one-fourth share.

related_chapters.subject: Carrie Lamar Young
related_chapters.chapter_title: Picking Cotton, and the Weighing Scale
related_chapters.why: Du Bois walks the cotton-field golden fleece and the scales that set the tenant's pay; Young recounts picking cotton and the weighing scale in the Delta fields.

related_chapters.subject: Mary Jones
related_chapters.why: The essay notes children pulled into the cotton harvest in place of schooling; Jones describes the child farm labor of picking cotton and pulling corn.

related_chapters.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Only Address I Ever Had
related_chapters.why: Du Bois traces lives spent in cabins on another man's plantation; Clark describes growing up in a house on the plantation that was the only address he ever had.

related_chapters.subject: Shirley Miller Sherrod
related_chapters.chapter_title: Picked Them Up in the 1870 Census
related_chapters.why: The essay closes on the rare Black freeholder who climbs from tenancy to owning land; Sherrod recounts a landowning farm family traceable in the 1870 census.

top_voice.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
top_voice.reason: Blake's account of the sharecropping debt that left a family owing its soul to the store is the closest living echo of the essay's argument about the crop-lien economy.

related_chapters.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller
related_chapters.why: Du Bois dissects the relations of master and man under the color line; the Millers describe the paternalism and the sharecropper's bargain that governed Black and white relations.

related_chapters.subject: Cecil J. Williams
related_chapters.why: The essay describes two worlds that mingle yet never meet across the color line; Williams recalls growing up in parallel Black and white societies in Orangeburg.

related_chapters.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
related_chapters.chapter_title: You Will Never Be My Equal
related_chapters.why: Du Bois examines the master's settled belief in the Negro's fixed place; Blake recounts plantation race relations summed up in being told he would never be an equal.

related_chapters.subject: Eddie Holloway
related_chapters.why: The essay argues the ballot is the dispossessed man's one defense; Holloway recounts the painstaking, counted-one-by-one effort to win the vote in Hattiesburg.

related_chapters.subject: Dion Diamond
related_chapters.part_title: What Was Lost, What Was Gained
related_chapters.why: Du Bois weighs what is lost and gained as the color line is contested across a generation; Diamond reflects on the bittersweet cost of desegregation.

related_chapters.subject: James Oscar Jones
related_chapters.part_title: Roots in Land, Church, and Fear
related_chapters.why: The essay shows how cunning and the scales kept the tenant in debt to master and merchant; Jones recounts a landowning family that weighed its own cotton against that bargain.

top_voice.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller
top_voice.reason: The Millers' account of paternalism and the sharecropper's bargain names the same master-and-man relations the essay sets out to describe plainly.

related_chapters.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
related_chapters.chapter_title: Adapting Church Songs Into Freedom Songs
related_chapters.why: Du Bois traces how the spirituals carried a people's faith and longing; the Carawans describe adapting church songs into the freedom songs of the movement.

related_chapters.subject: Jamila Jones
related_chapters.why: The essay hears the sorrow songs as a message sung from the heart; Jones recounts the songs, including This Little Light of Mine, that carried the movement's message.

related_chapters.subject: Pete Seeger
related_chapters.chapter_title: You Don't Have the Song Right
related_chapters.why: Du Bois follows a song's passage down the generations and into the wider world; Seeger traces the origins and traveling of We Shall Overcome.

related_chapters.subject: Euvester Simpson
related_chapters.topic: A Washcloth and a Gospel Song
related_chapters.why: The essay reads the spirituals as faith voiced amid suffering; Simpson recounts singing the gospel song Walk With Me, Lord through a night in the Winona jail.

related_chapters.subject: Juadine Henderson
related_chapters.why: Du Bois describes song as the people's collective voice rising in hope; Henderson recounts the freedom songs and the sense of freedom in the air at mass meetings.

related_chapters.subject: Walter Bruce
related_chapters.part_title: A Carpenter and a Gospel Singer
related_chapters.topic: The Gospel Group and Movement Music
related_chapters.why: The essay honors a sacred-song tradition carried across a lifetime; Bruce recounts fifty-four years of gospel singing tied to the movement.

top_voice.subject: Euvester Simpson
top_voice.reason: Simpson singing a gospel song through a night of jailhouse suffering embodies the essay's reading of the sorrow songs as faith voiced amid trouble and hope.

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.part_title: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
related_chapters.chapter_title: This One Will Go to College
related_chapters.topic: A Mother's Hope and a We
related_chapters.why: Du Bois insists on opening the college door to the striving few; Terry recounts a mother's hope that singled out one child to go to college.

related_chapters.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
related_chapters.why: The essay frames the Black college as the seedbed of trained leaders; Thelwell recounts arriving at Howard University in 1959.

related_chapters.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
related_chapters.why: Du Bois argues education must develop the whole person, not just a workman; Sellers reflects on the movement itself as a university that trained him.

related_chapters.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
related_chapters.chapter_title: It Does Not Have to Be This Way
related_chapters.why: Du Bois calls for a Talented Tenth of college-trained Black thinkers; Hrabowski recounts realizing the Meyerhoff Scholars program to produce Black PhDs in science.

related_chapters.subject: Glenda Funchess
related_chapters.why: The essay weighs the public schools as the chief means of training citizens; Funchess recounts the stair-step plan by which the schools began to desegregate.

related_chapters.subject: Timothy Jenkins
related_chapters.why: Du Bois ties higher training to leaders who think rather than untrained agitators; Jenkins describes Howard students taking on a more radical dimension as the sit-ins began.

top_voice.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
top_voice.reason: Hrabowski's lifework building a pipeline of college-trained Black scientists is the most direct modern realization of the essay's argument for higher education and a Talented Tenth.
